desc:
The slope slants down sharply to the west into a deep unforgiving looking
canyon. Along the exposed slope lie clay, rocks, and shale which do not offer
any adequate footing. To the east the ground is more level with some stones
dotting the surface while to the south lies a level area covered with thick and
tall vines.
name: Rocky Slide

desc:
The land begins to gradually slope downwards to the west and appears to become
much steeper farther west. Many stones, large and small, smooth and sharp, and
all manner in between create very unsure footing. To the east the ground smooths
out into a small clear area while the area to the south becomes very rocky.
name: Scattered Stones

desc:
The ground becomes clearer as it rises up from the forest and other lands to the
east and south. A few scattered rocks make the terrain a bit tricky as it
continues to rise to the west. But as the ground continues to rise further to
the west, the number and size of the rocks begins to get bigger.
name: A Small Rise

desc:
This area appears to have once been a tall and mighty forest. However, all that
is left now are the fallen and decayed trunks of this forest. Many of the trees
seem to have been felled outward, almost in a circular pattern, as if by a giant
windstorm. The splintered and burnt remains of other trees indicate that this
devastation was also caused by lightning.
name: Fallen Forest

desc:
Perhaps at one time this area may have been thickly wooded, but now, only a few
lonely trees stand as a reminder of what this area once was. Fallen trunks and
decayed branches lie scattered among the broken stumps. Long grasses cover much
of the rest of the ground.
name: Cleared Woodland

desc:
The landscape levels out into a smooth sea of tall grasses. Save for the tops of
the grasses swaying to the whims of the wind, little else can be seen. The plain
continues far into the east while the ground to the west rises a little, and
numerous tree stumps can be seen. To the south the grasses become very high and
visibility in that direction is limited.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Broad grasslands extend far into the eastern and western horizon. Millions of
grass blades softly swish and sway, appearing as if they were trying to keep
from falling down. To the south, the wide flat expanse of the grasslands gives
way to barren and uneven ground.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The thick grasses create a solid green plain that sprawls into the horizon. The
flat expanse of the prairie, however, is dominated overhead by the wide open
sky. To the south the solid green of the grasses abruptly changes to a charred
blackness.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
You are surrounded by low, evergreen bushes growing in the thin soil on these
rocky hills. Outcroppings of white stone give the land an ancient, exhausted
appearance. Here, in a swale between two hills, the bedrock has been completely
denuded in an oval patch about a third of a mile long, and the white stone has
been discolored as if a fire raged here long ago. Even where mud and soggy old
leaves have collected, nothing grows in this patch. There is no sign of human
life, and little evidence of large game. The land slopes upward to the north and
east.
name: Scorched Heath

desc:
The treeless land slopes upward to the north and east. The hard, stony ground is
covered by a carpet of heath and heathery plants about six inches tall.
Underneath it the soil is very thin and barely covers the rotten and crumbling
limestone that lies beneath. They say this land was once forested. That must
have been very long ago.
name: Heath-covered Slope

desc:
The flat land here is maybe two hundred feet higher than the valley of the
Glanduin a few miles to the south. The soil is very thin here, and the rock
underneath it is old crumbling limestone, which protrudes through holes in its
purplish-green blanket of heath here and there, like exposed bones in an eroded
graveyard. There is no sign that this land has ever been inhabited. It is silent
and empty.
name: Heath-covered Highland

desc:
The ground here seems generally flat, but travelling over it one discovers
numerous irritating crevices, holes, depressions, and stony obstructions, all
more or less hidden by an eight-inch blanket of grey-green heath. The bone-
coloured limestone of these hills is overlain here by a thin layer of harder
greyish rock. Millennia of erosion have undercut the harder stone and caused it
to crack apart and crumble into sharp-edged chunks, which lie scattered about
here under the vegetation ready to turn the ankles of the passer-by or cause his
horse to stumble and rear. But there is no sign of anyone having passed this way
since the creation of the world.
name: Rocky Highland

desc:
The rocky heath-covered land slopes gently downward to the south here. The
ground is strewn with sharp-sided chunks of stone the size of a man's head,
which are largely concealed by the foot-high heather which grows everywhere
here. The soil is very thin; the heath seems to be rooted in the cracks of the
stone itself. There are no obvious watercourses here - it seems as if the
rainwater washes evenly off the old rock of the slope, or sinks into the rotten
limestone and disappears.
name: Rocky Slope

desc:
The land slopes evenly downward from west to east. The ground is stone,
liberally covered with an evergreen carpet, a tangle of deep-green leaves, woody
stems, and tiny white and lavender flowers. A few large rocky fragments are
buried in the heath, lying where they have rolled down from the higher ground to
the west.
name: Heath-covered Slope

desc:
Bushes and small trees, many with fragrant wood and flowers, and most with nasty
thorns, cover the rocky land here; mud and sand from a recent flood form a flat
surface which readily reveals tracks. The rustling of small animals, and the
fluid calls of songbirds, are heard frequently from the brush - generally, at
some distance from you.
name: Brushy Riverbank

desc:
This is a broad north-south valley choked with sand and pebbles. A very shallow
stream meanders southward through it. Its course is convoluted, being more like
a network of connected pools than a single stream. Other shallow pools lie as
far as two hundred yards from the main course of the stream. It is clear that
most of the flow of water through this valley is of ground water seeping
southward through the porous sand and gravel, and that the stream and pools
observed on the surface are only a small fraction of the total. The valley is
bounded by brushy banks about six feet high, undercut here and there by flood
waters; but gravel bars abound, and it is not hard to find a place to climb the
banks.
name: Forglanduin Sands

desc:
The topsoil here is thin, supporting only small and scattered clumps of rough
grass. Deep rills running north and west cut the surface, exposing shards of
bone-white limestone to the elements. To the west the plateau dips sharply,
dropping to a brush filled valley roughly eighty feet wide.
name: Plateau

desc:
The ground here is flat enough, though it drops off very sharply toward the
river valley to the west. The topsoil is very thin, and only sparse clumps of
coarse grass can be found here. It looks as if most of the rain that falls here
runs off rapidly, sinking into the porous rock or pouring down into the valley
through normally dry washes.
name: Plateau

desc:
Low, thorny brush and stiff brown clumps of grass cover a rocky landscape. The
land is generally flat, dropping off sharply toward the river to the west, but
it is cut by narrow, steep-walled ravines where floods have washed away the
rotten underlying rock, and travelling is difficult. A barely perceptible
footpath runs through the uninviting landscape from north to south along the lip
of the embankment which falls toward the river.
name: Plateau

desc:
This is an area of brush on an earthen bluff overlooking a river to the east.
Much of the brush has been trampled down or dug up, though. To the south you can
clearly see some structures, which have been covered with mud and thorny
branches, and walled around by the same, to the point where they are almost
impossible to make out from any direction but your own. The mud here seems
exhausted, barely fertile.
name: Entrance to a Hidden Village

desc:
A rocky stream splashes downward to the west, meandering across the bottom of a
canyon fifty feet deep and a hundred feet wide. The water is low, and the stream
can be forded or crossed on stones at many points. Occasionally you may hear a
fish splashing in one of the deeper pools.
name: Muddy Tributary

desc:
Low hills extend southward and eastward from here, with no visible sign of
habitation. It seems certain that animals do graze on this short grass - their
hoofprints are all around - but there is no sign of crofter or herdsman. Flies
buzz, the wind sighs, and far overhead a bird gives a mournful squawk. This is a
silent land.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
A stony bluff overlooks the flat terrain below from an elevation of sixty feet
or so. It is composed mainly of stone which the rain has not yet worn away; what
little soil is left supports a coarse, scraggly growth of short grass. Signs
that grazing animals have been here are abundant.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
These are low hills, nearly barren, covered by a tenuous coating of poor soil
and short grass. They rise only a hundred feet or so above the wetlands to the
west, and have been heavily eroded by rain and wind. They seem tired, old, as if
waiting for a new change of the world to wash them away.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
The low ground here has been flooded by the water of the river, and tall reeds
grow in the shallow water. Some old, rotten tree trunks lie among them, evidence
that this has not always been a wetland. They are rapidly decaying, though; and
frogs splash, blue flies buzz, watersnakes slither through the reeds as if this
had been a marsh since the creation of the world.
name: Reeds

desc:
A compromise between the river to the north and the great reed-beds to the
south, this area of shallow water is dotted with clumps of vegetation and water
plants, laying their purple-dotted leaves gently on the surface. Water- loving
insects dart about, barely rippling the surface as their featherweight bodies
alight on the water, seeking some tiny morsel of food.
name: Shallow Water

desc:
The Forglanduin meanders south and west toward its junction with the Glanduin,
its shallow waters placid and brown. Along its banks small animals peek out from
the brush and reeds, watching for fish or water birds. Travel is slow due to the
mud and wet sand of the broad riverbed.
name: Forglanduin River

desc:
Here the Forglanduin is freshened by a tributary which joins it from the east.
The flow of the combined streams, however, is not so great as to pose a serious
obstacle to travel; the water is easily forded on any of numerous gravel bars
and rock shelves. Piles of matted brush and branches line the edges of the
floodplain.
name: Forglanduin River

desc:
The river winds southward through a flat floodplain about two miles wide. In its
present low-water state it is in effect a series of pools linked by an easily
wadeable brownish flow. There seem to be few fish; birds of prey circle overhead
but find nothing to stoop upon. The land is silent and empty.
name: Forglanduin River

desc:
The shallow, muddy river trickles southward through low, apparently uninhabited
brush-covered terrain, winding back and forth across a level flood plain about a
mile and a half wide. Steep, undercut banks bound that plain, and driftwood,
debris, and mud have been deposited here and there in cluttered heaps.
name: Forglanduin River

desc:
Here the Forglanduin is a shallow, brown stream meandering southward across a
broad valley. In time of flood the water probably fills the entire valley,
undercutting the tall brushy banks you see to the east and west. Now the water
is low, and the exposed sand and mud banks are speckled with green weeds.
name: Forglanduin River

desc:
Close-knit thorny shrubs and bushes make it tough going on this gentle slope
leading down to a watercourse. Exotic orange-and-white flowers grow on some of
the fragrant boughs. A stand of briars attracts your attention.
name: Brushy Riverbank

desc:
Thorny branches and spiny canes are woven together into a solid mass which makes
free movement all but impossible, exept where someone or some group of game
animals has left narrow trails through and among the shrubs. Sounds of small
scurrying animals and flapping birds can be heard down under the branches.
name: Brushy Riverbank

desc:
This is the western edge of the silt-covered floodplain of the Forglanduin, and
because (in contrast with the land eastward) there is actually some soil
overlying the bedrock, some thick vegetation has sprung up. The bushes are
covered with waxy green leaves, and in the summertime they bloom with colourful
blossoms an inch or more across.
name: Brushy Riverbank

desc:
Shrubs bearing thick, deep-green leaves and nasty spines grow thickly here, but
travel is possible where the foliage and canes have been trampled by wild game.
The ground is soggy mud dotted with hard rocky protrusions.
name: Brushy Riverbank

desc:
The shallow waters of the Forglanduin spread out here over a wide plain,
seemingly as flat as a tabletop. Reeds hide the contours of the river's bank;
the river is braided into several channels separated by pristine tan sand bars.
Fish splash in the deeper pools, and the gargling cries of game birds are heard
from the marshlands.
name: Forglanduin River

desc:
The brown waters of the Forglanduin have pooled here and spread over a broad
expanse of the countryside, too deep to touch bottom. There is little current
here. To the south the river is overshadowed by a great stone structure marching
east and west - the ancient dwarven causeway, carrying the road that once linked
the ancient centres of wealth and power in Middle-earth.
name: Forglanduin River

desc:
The great dwarven road follows the course of the Glanduin here as it meanders
west and north. Downstream, far to the west, is the city of Tharbad, once a
great Numenorean seaport and the recipient of the commerce of Eregion and Moria.
Since then the coasts have been changed, and Tharbad lies far inland; Numenor,
Eregion, and Moria have all been ruined, and the road is nearly deserted.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The great elevated structure marches north and south, following the River
Glanduin curve for curve. Built to carry the commerce of Eregion when that land
was Middle-earth's most glorious realm, today it bestrides the empty mud flats
in this silent and deserted landscape.
name: On the Great Causeway

desc:
You are on a monumental structure of stone, a great causeway elevated upon
columns of black marble and carrying an ancient highway over the floodplains of
the Glanduin and Forglanduin. Built by the dwarves five thousand years ago and
more to carry the commerce of Eregion, it stands today, battered but strong, a
monument to the vanished power of the dwarves of Khazad-dum, to the departed
wealth of the elven lords of now-ruined Ost-in-Edhil. The road curves from the
west toward the south here.
name: On the Great Causeway

desc:
The road follows the bend of the river from east to north as it has for
thousands of years, but recent years have been unkind and the few paving stones
which show through the grass and weeds are worn and cracked by the elements. To
the west, the Glanduin flows steadily onward towards its meeting with the
Mitheithel near Tharbad. The south bank is covered with holly and bushes, but in
the distance you can see the plains of Enedwaith stretching as far as the eye
can see.
name: The Road of the Smiths

desc:
Here the road has been reduced to about two thirds of its former width by the
encroachment of the river and the southern side may not be very stable for more
than a man on foot. To the east the road continues, more or less safe from the
inexorable flow of the river to the west. There are a few bushes along the side
of the bank there and thus the road is holding up much better. The south bank of
the river appears to be covered with a dense bushy growth which is mostly holly.
name: The Road of the Smiths

desc:
Here, where great piers carry the ancient Road of the Smiths over the main
channel of the Forglanduin, the structure shows unusual signs of wear and
damage. The stone columns have largely collapsed on the north side of the
pavement; although the great slabs upholding the pavement have not collapsed,
they are tilted dangerously at crazy angles. A great mound of heavy debris -
willow trunks, great roots, and mud - has been piled against and under the span;
from below you, you hear a booming sound, a sound of falling, churning water.
name: Ruined Bridge on the Causeway

desc:
Sixty feet above the mud flats of the Forglanduin the great old dwarven road
marches on columns of stone, defying time and the river's waters alike. On
either side of the road the land is flat as a sheet of paper, an expanse of
marshes and standing water, scoured by the flood water which rolls off the
almost soilless heath. On the north side of the causeway debris lies thick where
the floods have hurled it.
name: The Great Causeway

desc:
The road continues east and west atop its levee, twice the height of a man above
the flat land to the north of the road and the riverbank to the south. The dark
stones set here by the dwarves five thousand years ago are still firmly in place
and mostly smooth. This road was once packed with trade to and from the dwarven
dwellings at faraway Khazad-dum; but the collapse of the North-kingdom and the
awakening of Durin's Bane have left the road desolate.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The ancient dwarven road continues east and west, elevated above the surround-
ing floodplain country on an earthen levee. Here, however, the road is covered
with a thin layer of wet clay. Water has frequently overflowed the road from the
north side, running over the pavement, eroding the levee on the south side, and
leaving a deposit of sediment behind.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The elevated stone road continues east and west, straight as an arrow. Grass and
small weeds grow from the dirt which has collected between the stones. Still, it
seems nearly as sound as when it was built thousands of years ago. There is very
little sign that anyone still uses this road, however.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
You are on a smooth, straight, and ancient road. Tightly-fitted stones make up
the pavement, which is raised above the surrounding plain on an embankment the
height of a man. East and west it proceeds, parallel to the River Glanduin which
meanders south of the road, now a mile distant, now lapping just below you at
the foot of a breakwater. Once this was a busy highway; today the road and the
land around it are desolate.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The road appears to be solid and well engineered despite its obvious lack of use
and maintenance. A noticeable layer of dust has settled over the large cobbles
of the road and the stone blocks lining its sides. The rocks comprising the
surface of the road appear smoothed and flattened from age and wear. The route
continues east and west running parallel to the river in the south.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The road slopes downwards as it dips closer to the river in the south. The large
stones which fit together to comprise this road seem to have buckled a bit,
possibly indicating that this area may have sunk somewhat since the road's
construction.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
As the road follows the river on its course, a natural steep ledge forms between
the road and the river to the south. Apparently the river has eroded its bed
deep into the ground causing a cliff like wall to form from the road down to its
banks. The large stones lining the road provide a nice overlook onto the river's
waters as well as helping to prevent any careless falls down into the water.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The solid and hard route continues to the east and the west. The road, itself,
does not show any ill effects from the ages of wear it probably has endured.
Each stone on the road seems to have been laid with great care, and together
they all fit perfectly with one another. Despite their apparent age, no cracks
have formed between the stone blocks and the wear over the years only seems to
help smooth it out.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The large flat stones of the road pave the way east and west through the wild
lands of Eregion. A small grassy clearing by the side of the road to the south
allows a spectacular view of the rushing river. A short distance to the north, a
large ironwood tree stands, offering shelter underneath its branches. A large
headstone can be seen under the thick boughs of the tree.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The rows of the stones lining the road bend causing the route to run east and
south. The surface of the road, though dusty and hard, remains flat and
consistent. To the west a path can be seen leading from the road into a large
formation of rock cliffs while thick bushes hide the area to the north.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The flat dusty road continues north and south. Despite the dust and natural
debris collecting on its sides, the roadway remains in good shape, its rocks
seeming solid and firm on the ground. The river can be seen flowing to the east
while a glint coming from the surface of a lake can be seen in the southwest.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The Road of the Smiths crosses the River Glanduin. The bridge looks very old and
sturdy. In the middle of the bridge, there is a trapdoor that must lead down to
the river below the bridge. The river flows under the bridge from east to west.
The distance from the bridge down to the water is too far for anyone to risk
jumping into the water. The road continues north and south from the bridge.
name: Bridge over the River Glanduin

desc:
A long bend in the river leads it from north to west. To the west an old bridge
made of wood and stone lets the river pass beneath a road going from north to
south. On the southern side of the river bend plains filled with grass and
flowers lie just above the riverbank, while on the eastern side lots of small
bushes grow close together. Further east some trees can be seen in the distant
horizon.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
This shallow part of the lake is at the north-eastern end. At the northern side
sand cover the shore, while at the eastern side the lake ends in a river running
beneath a bridge. The river Glanduin exits into the lake here. The bottom of the
lake here is soft, as it is mostly sand covering it. A small current runs
westwards from here because of the water entering the lake from Glanduin.
name: Wide Lake

desc:
From here the road continues north and south. Further south it bends westwards,
while north of here it crosses a bridge. The ground is covered with dirt, and
hardly no stones at all. A few, old footprints of horses and men can be noticed
as the ground is searched thoroughly. Westwards a large lake covers the area
north of the road, while to the east a grassy plain appears.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The road bends north and west from here. To the west the road seems to continue
on forever, while northwards a bridge appears in the distance. Below the bridge
a river flows out into the lake that lies north-west of here. South of the road
lies a small stream that also runs out into the wide lake in the north-east. A
small path leaves the road eastwards from here, leading down to the stream.
name: Bend in the Road

desc:
A sturdy stone bridge makes crossing the stream comfortable. Below the bridge, a
trickling stream flows from the south to the north, ending up in the wide lake
to the north. The Road of the Smiths continues to the east and west. There is a
trapdoor on the middle of the bridge that seems to lead down under the bridge.
name: Bridge over a Small Stream

desc:
At the south-eastern end of this lake the water is clear and light blue. The
ground at the bottom of the lake is filled with small stones which makes it
rather pleasant to move around. Along the shore a road runs over a bridge to the
south and continues northwards along the eastern shore. A small stream runs
southwards beneath the bridge and on into the distance.
name: Wide Lake

desc:
This path leads southwards down to the bank of a small stream, and westwards to
a road that bends north and west. East and north from the path plains cover the
area as far as the eye can see. The plains are filled with medium long grass,
that is quite dense. In between the grass tall flowers grow, and some trees lie
scattered about.
name: Path to a Stream

desc:
The small path winds it way south along the side of the stream that flows west
of here. Short grass covers the middle of the path, while longer grass grows on
the sides of the path and down towards the water. This path leads to a road
north-west from here. To the south-east lies a hill, and beyond the hill a
large, beautiful lake appears into the view.
name: Path along the Stream

desc:
The small path continues along the stream on the eastern bank. The stream
continues south, while the path ends here. There is a small strip of grass
between the path and the water. South of the path a tall hill rises above the
ground. East of the path the land is very flat, covered with spots of tall,
yellow grass. Apart from these spots, nothing seems to grow there at all.
name: Path along the Stream

desc:
The road continues along the bank of the lake east and west. A bridge can be
seen letting the road pass over a small stream east of here. The bridge is old,
but sturdy, and made of wood and stone. Southwards small, but dense, bushes
cover the area with their long thorns, while to the north a huge lake lies with
its cold dark blue water.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
Continuing in a rather straight line from east to west the old road winds its
way through the wasted landscape. While the road gets more and more overgrown as
you move west, it becomes more visible to the east. Scattered along the road you
notice some holly bushes. North of here a dense thicket prevents you from
continuing that way, while south of here lies a dusty plain. East of here the
road continues.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The Road of the Smiths bends here, and continues to the north and west. This
part of the road seems seldom used. The area is covered with patches of weeds.
The bushes to the east are too dense to allow passage through them. The only way
through must be to avoid the nasty looking thorns. To the south plains stretch
out in every direction.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
Dense bushes cover the area east of the road. To the north beyond the road a
lake can be seen in the horizon. The road continues north and south from here,
rocky and bumpy. This part of the road seems to be seldom used, but some
footprints of people, and a few tracks of horses can be noticed as the ground is
searched thouroughly.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The Road of Smiths continue south and east from here, while an old, seldom used
road continues westwards. The Road of Smiths, wide with the ground consisting
mostly of dirt, seems to have been used recently. To the north a large lake
covers most of the horizon from north to north-east. Rocks and deep water makes
it hard to access the water from here, although the lake is almost level with
the road.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
This road shows signs of decaying. It is mostly overgrown by high grass and
large bushes. Some of them have big thorns and you have to walk carefully to
protect your clothes and skin. The road was once paved with flat stones, but now
they are mostly broken and scattered but they still show the course of the road.
To the east this road meets the Road of the Smiths which is in a much better
condition. You see the remains of a high building to the north along the road.
To the west and south the ground becomes softer and you can see small pools of
smelly water.
name: Overgrown Road

desc:
Here the road ends at the edge of a river. The river is rather wide here and it
looks like it has a strong current. At the riverbank you can see the broken
remains of pillars that once supported a bridge, but now there seems to be no
way across it. Looking northwards you can also see a large high hill. To the
west lies a decayed stone building with a large tower attached to it. It seems
to be very old. Southwards the old road continues while east of here lies a
lake.
name: End of Road

desc:
The road continues west and east from here, while to the north a vast lake
covers the horizon. This road mostly consists of dirt and small stones. A few
tracks can be seen on the ground, as some people on horses passed the area
lately. South of the road lies an area with dense, thorny bushes that looks
unpleasant to move around in.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The land slopes evenly south and west toward the Glanduin. The soil is thin and
infertile, and the vegetation consists of coarse grass and prickly plants,
growing in drab clumps. The ground seems tired, ruined, worn out. There are no
trees in sight, no bushes, no plants higher than a man's boot-tops.
name: Treeless Slope

desc:
The hills here slope slightly to the south and west. Rising about eighty feet
above the surrounding wetlands, the top of these hills is on a level with the
stand of oak trees to the west. Trickling busily around, between and over rocks
and patches of sand and shale a small stream cuts its way down towards these
trees. The soil here seems to be worn out; spread thinly and looking tired it
supports very little vegetation.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
The water of the Forglanduin has collected here in a deep lake two miles across.
This is because its free passage under the main span of the ancient causeway to
the south has been obstructed by a great pile of debris which has collected
against the columns - tree trunks, brush, mud, even the carcasses of boar and
sheep. Although the surface of the water seems calm, there is a slow current
coaxing you in the direction of the causeway, where you can see twigs and leaves
on the surface swirl in a powerful eddy.
name: Forglanduin River

desc:
The ground here is soggy, and your footprints fill with standing water; the
brush here is sickly and in danger of rotting, while plants more typical of wet
ground have sprung up everywhere. Frogs can be heard croaking, screeching, and
splashing in every direction.
name: Marshy Ground

desc:
Thick bushes covers the ground making it hard to travel through. It slopes down
to the west, towards a flooded valley. Small flowers hides under the bushes,
they grow in blue and purple.
name: Brushy Ground

desc:
Here a shallow swale, the valley of a small stream which runs down from the low
hills to the north here, has been filled with murky water, forming a broad lake.
There do not seem to be any fish living in it. Around its edges the vegetation
is soggy and rotten.
name: Flooded Valley

desc:
Tall, thorny bushes with thick leaves and aromatic twigs grow densely here, and
passage is difficult. It is impossible to see very far in any direction.
Occasionally you hear the scampering of a small animal running for safety in the
brush.
name: Brush

desc:
Thick heath grows the height of a man here, mixed with dense thorny shrubs and
briars whose interlaced shoots make travel difficult and unpleasant. Small
animals can be heard scurrying for cover as you pass through. Here and there are
signs that someone has come this way on a horse, with most of the hoofprints
pointing north or south. There is no sign that anyone lives in this land,
though. Occasionally you see a wheeling hawk abruptly stoop and then soar away
with a tiny squirming shape in its talons.
name: Thick Brush

desc:
The ground slopes up to form a slight and broad rise. The soil here is ingrained
with jagged rocks which appear to have sunk into the ground over time. The rise
leaves the area exposed to the elements which keep the rise bare of growth. To
the north, the ground levels out into a vast grassland while a road can be seen
to the south.
name: Rugged Transition

desc:
A few pines grow on the rocky ground here. A faint trail running north and south
is imprinted on the hard plain soil. The rock strewn ground spreads all about,
but is met by a large distinct looking road to the south. It appears that the
trail connects to the road and runs north, far into the flat sparse woods to the
north.
name: Faint Path

desc:
Thick bristling pines stand tall on the level ground, occupying much of the
landscape. Sparse grasses cover the ground while decaying pine needles darken
the ground beneath the pine branches. To the north, a stone building can been
seen nestled among the trees.
name: Wooded Plains

desc:
This house appears to have been standing for a long time from the look of the
walls which show much wear. Nonetheless, the walls remain very sturdy due to
their design which included hefty stones. The carpentry does not look dwarvish
as there are no chiselled nor hammered features within the house, which, itself,
has a much more natural look. The stones are round and smooth, and vines climb
up the walls near the windows. The light making its way inside grants a calm,
tranquil effect to the room. A large table spans the entire northern wall with
some miscellaneous items still remaining on its surface. Even the wooden planked
floor looks solid despite the significant layer of dust and a few pronounced
scratches.
name: Inside an Abandoned House

desc:
The plain of grass shimmers as the tips of the grass blades sway and bend back
and forth. The grass is dense, making passage a difficult and tiring task. To
the west, a large chunk of rock can be seen standing in the distance. To the
east the monotony of the green plains abruptly changes into a multitude of
colourful flowers sprouting in an open field. To the south there appears to be a
small dwelling, made of stone and wood.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The flat even ground of the plains sinks down into a wide though very slight
depression. A few dark green bushes are situated near the centre of the basin
and seem to be encircling a small pool of water. Except to the north, tall
grasses on higher ground prevent any view of the surrounding area. In the north,
the ground appears to be very rocky and little grass grows there.
name: Shallow Basin

desc:
This stretch of ground is, for the most part, barren. The soil is coarse and
uneven, giving the area a rough and rocky appearance. Sharp dips and steep hills
warp and pockmark the ground in sharp contrast to the flat grassy plains to the
north. Here and there, hostile looking brownish thornbushes dot the bare ground.
name: Rough Ground

desc:
In comparison to the flourishing plains to north and south, the ground here
seems to be dead. Burnt ashes cover much of the ground, darkening the land and
giving it a somber aspect. Though there is no smell on the ashes, the overall
lack of any resumed growth in the ground seems to indicate that fire swept
through this area not too long ago.
name: Blackened Soil

desc:
The clay soil here is very thin; in many places the white, chalky rock beneath
pokes through like an elbow through a threadbare sleeve. The vegetation consists
of low, evergreen, flowering bushes growing amid scraggly clumps of coarse
grass. The land is uneven, rising and falling in abrupt little steps, like a
thin silken sheet spread over scattered bricks.
name: Heath

desc:
The land here slopes downward toward the west. The thin soil and the low,
evergreen bushes that grow in it cover the hillside like old, worn burlap; the
underlying limestone can be seen poking through it in little outcroppings
everywhere.
name: Heath-covered Slope

desc:
This heath-covered hill rises about a hundred feet above the valleys to the
southeast and southwest, and provides a good view of the landscape. It extends
below you like a purplish-green blanket thrown on the ground for a picnic: low,
green heath on low, stream-worn hillocks. Once great old trees grew here in
lush, rich earth; now the trees, and most of the soil, are gone perhaps forever.
name: Treeless Hill

desc:
All around you are miles of treeless heath-covered moorland. The soil is thin to
nonexistent; large rocks, forced to the surface by a thousand freezes and thaws,
lie about everywhere and are being covered in turn by lichens and the
omnipresent heath and heather. The land slopes gently down to the east here.
name: Rocky Slope

desc:
This is a broad, shallow bowl-shaped depression in the treeless, heath-covered
landscape. A little north of its centre, a spring burbles out from under a rocky
shelf, forming a small clear puddle. The stream that begins here meanders off
toward the south and west; at the moment there is not enough flow from the
spring, however, to maintain the stream's flow.
name: Spring at the Head of a Stream

desc:
The rounded crest of a ridge runs from north to south here, covered in a
seamless blanket of heath; it resembles a gentle fold in a drapery of deep green
cloth. Tiny lavender flowers speckle the landscape, blending at a distance with
the pine-green hue of the heath and giving it a shadowy tint. The rock of the
ridge is near the surface, but has been smoothed by years, and centuries of rain
and wind. Little animals move here and there - a snake, a field mouse - but
overall the land seems very quiet, as if it were exhausted.
name: Low, Heath-covered Ridge

desc:
A heavily eroded ridge curving from the north to the southwest rises above the
more level heathlands on either side. A few fragments of rock, almost buried in
the thick tangle of woody stems and foliage, mark the rounded crest. To the
east, the land drops more steeply toward a tangle of brush. There are no trees,
signs of large game, or relics of the days gone by when men came here in great
numbers to fell the seemingly inexhaustible forests.
name: Low, Heath-covered Ridge

desc:
The land is rather even here, sloping very gently eastward toward a line of
brush. The ground is essentially bare stone, covered with a sprinkling of humus
and a seamless carpet of heath. The dark green leaves and pale pink and purple
flowers are the only things saving the landscape from complete dreariness.
name: Gentle Slope

desc:
The brush-covered ground slopes southward here, and the lower ground is wetter
and almost marshy. Bushes and thorny shrubs grow thickly here, their leaves
thick, deep green, and glossy, their roots gnarled and heavy, and their thorns
sharp and waxy. Fragrant flowers bloom on the trees. Birds wheel overhead, then
stoop on their unseen prey.
name: Brushy Slope

desc:
This wetland extends eastward across a flat floodplain. At best you are wading
in a foot of water, surrounded by reeds higher than a man. There are also
numerous deeper holes, open pools, and mires, and you must watch your steps
carefully. Dragonflies dart soundlessly among the tall blades, and wading birds
stalk through the water, standing still, then plunging their long beaks below
the surface after a frog or a minnow.
name: Tall Reeds

desc:
Shrubs and trees grow here, low and spreading, few taller than a tall man. Their
resinous thorny twigs interlace and make a mass of brush and briars, difficult
to penetrate. Their leaves are grey-green and the flowers are aromatic. The soil
is thin muddy sand over an uneven rocky layer which protrudes here and there in
outcroppings crowned with scraggly bushes. Small, swift animals and birds scurry
through the undergrowth.
name: Brushland

desc:
The heath-covered landscape slopes gently downward to the west here. Around you
is a colony of white-flowered plants; the hills around you are covered with the
more common purple-flowered variety. A few stones the size of a man lie here.
name: Gentle Slope

desc:
You are in the valley of a tiny stream which runs down from the higher ground to
the north. There is not much water in its bed, but it is enough to give life to
a dense tangle of bushes with thick aromatic leaves and thorny canes. Small
animals scurry away from your noisy passage. To the south the ground is moister.
name: Brushy Valley

desc:
The heath-covered, soil-poor land slopes gently southward toward the distant
Glanduin. Small streambeds, which probably contain water only during the spring
rains, cut the surface of the rocky hills.
name: Heath-covered Land

desc:
The land is flat here, and the soil fertile, and tall grass grows all around you
in a green wavy sea. Its tops ripple with each breath of air. Tall stalks
covered by gay conical heads of blue flowers poke up among the grass, competing
for the attention of the bees. Your passage stirs up rustling movements in the
grass as small, unseen creatures fast scurry away from you. Occasionally you
tread upon stone or rotten wood and, looking down between the stalks, you can
discern the remnants of a foundation, a fence rail, a cart - memories of the
time when the farmers of Cardolan made this place a granary, before that kingdom
was desolated by war and plague centuries ago. Here and there the grass has been
beaten down more recently, and you can see hoofprints - sometimes headed south,
sometimes headed east.
name: Tall Grass

desc:
Wild healthy grasses grow all around, rising high and thick. Occasional breaks
in the tall grasses provide glimpses of more grassland to the north while the
tops of some trees can be seen in the western horizon. The grass is especially
thick to the south, preventing any view in that direction. A trampled narrow
trail leads south through the grass.
name: Grass Sea

desc:
Hiding any trace of the solid ground, a layer of tall grass shimmers silently,
creating the appearance of a soft green surface. The grass plain extends into
the distance all about, completely encompassing the landscape, only finally
giving way to the open sky in the horizon. To the west there appears to be a
small dwelling, made of stone and wood.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
This is the southern slope of a low hill which is covered by thick clumps of
heather. Its myriad minute bell-shaped flowers dance as you pass. The soil is
thin and poor, and outcroppings of the underlying white limestone can be seen
everywhere.
name: Heath-covered Hillside

desc:
You are on a rocky slope facing generally eastward, covered with the low, green,
tough brush characteristic of the heathland. The soil is poor, but small animals
and game birds can be seen if you look carefully, hiding from you and from any
great birds of prey that might fly overhead in this open country.
name: Heath

desc:
Two tiny stream beds, one from the northeast and one from the northwest, meet
here creating a single stream with a visible trickle of water in it. The stream
is almost obscured by the thick thorny brush which has grown up around it. The
shrubs have thick leaves and aromatic stems which produce a pungent, spicy odour
as you laboriously force your way through them.
name: Brushy Valley

desc:
Low evergreen woody plants cover an expanse of very low rocky hummocks here,
sloping upward imperceptibly toward the southeast. The squeaks and chatters of
rabbits, grouse, and other small animals can be heard, but the animals do their
best to stay out of your sight. A hawk wheels high overhead, surveying the empty
landscape. Elves, men, and dwarves have lived in this land or travelled through
it, but you see little evidence of their presence.
name: Heath

desc:
Here low shrubs with waxy green leaves interlace their thorny branches into a
dense mass, making travel very difficult! The twigs which break off as you pass
exude a pleasant fragrance. The ground is flat, and stony outcroppings can be
seen among the roots of the brushy plants.
name: Brush

desc:
You are on a low north-south ridge, barren of trees, poor in soil, and covered
by thick clumps of woody-stemmed evergreen plants with tiny purple flowers. A
flat stone shelf is at the summit of one of the stony hummocks, and may once
have been the foundation of a building of some kind, hundreds or thousands of
years ago; this is the only sign that people have ever lived in this land.
name: Heath

desc:
A streambed runs southward here, a waterless furrow in the centre of a shallow
valley; perhaps after heavy rains there is even water in it. Near the streams
course the heath is taller and bushier, and there are even a few shrubs of alder
and juniper, their roots straggling over the rocks and grasping for purchase.
name: Valley in the Heath

desc:
This is actually not so much a ridge as an uneroded tongue of rock between two
small stream beds. Low evergreen leafy bushes cover the gentle slope, ankle-high
where the soil is particularly thin, knee-high elsewhere. The deep green heath
is bedecked with tiny purple and white flowers, and bees and hummingbirds can be
glimpsed as bright flashes of colour which dance about and then vanish.
name: On a Low Ridge

desc:
You are surrounded by heath, lush but tough bushes with small, evergreen leaves
and tiny pink flowers that rise up taller than a man in the densest clumps.
Occasionally a thorny briar tears at you. The land is generally flat and a
small, waterless streambed winds eastward, lined with denser brush and an
occasional willow tree.
name: Heath

desc:
This is the northern slope of a broad, low hill. It is a mass of cracked white
stone, broken apart by fissures which radiate from the point where you stand.
Little grows on the exposed stone, and where the wind has blown leaves and
debris into the cracks, rain has washed them out again, so the land has not
recovered well from whatever event took place here.
name: Cracked Hillside

desc:
Bright hues and deep tints catch the eye, a welcome break from the solid green
of the endless plains. Scatterings of white, blue, red, yellow, and even purple
in the form of flowers grow all about. The ground appears as if a rainbow had
collided with Arda at this very spot and had penetrated the soil, causing this
sweet confusion.
name: Flowered Meadow

desc:
Lush grass carpets the land, creating a solid green surface over the ground. The
long thin blades of the wild grass rise up several hands high, easily obscuring
anything shorter than a hobbit. To the north, the green grass of the plains
quickly turns into dark ashes of burnt ground.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
This is a broad, shallow valley with a faint watercourse draining to the south-
east. There is more soil here than on the hills to the north but it is a heavy
clay, not terribly fertile, and the tough grass grows in thick clumps. The
watercourse is marked by a line of thick bushes and some low, thick-boled oak
trees. From time to time you see a bird of prey wheeling silently high above
you.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The land here slopes generally downward to the southwest, and is covered by
tough, scrubby evergreen bushes which in places are as tall as a horse. Riding
through the heath must be an odd experience, almost like swimming in a
grey-green sea. Although the land seems completely deserted now, it was once
productive farmland, and a few stones of an old farmhouse foundation can be seen
providing shade for those plants that need it. To the west of it are some plants
with lacy, dissected leaves and broad heads of tiny white flowers. You may have
even heard that this land was all forest once, but that seems hardly credible.
name: Heath-covered Slope

desc:
The land here slopes very gently southward. Exposed stones are everywhere, but a
thick carpet of heath and bracken covers the rocky shelves and softens their
outlines. The dartings of small birds and game among the ground cover can be
perceived from the corner of your eye.
name: Heath-covered Slope

desc:
A treeless landscape surrounds you on all sides. The bedrock has been all but
denuded of soil, and thousands of years of runoff have worn steep-sided furrows
in the low rocky hillocks. Yet the thick blanket of heath which covers hill and
valley alike has softened the edges of this otherwise harsh terrain. When the
sun is low and red, its rays arouse splashes of gaiety and life from the flowers
of the heath, bringing a few minutes of beauty to this most ancient and abused
land.
name: Heath

desc:
Great, large clumps of pink-flowered heather have sprung up here on a gently
undulating westward-trending slope. There is not a tree to be seen for miles.
The ground is hard; the bedrock is only a few inches below the surface.
name: Among the Heather

desc:
Tall golden grass sways around you, almost caressing your legs as it bends to
and fro, moved by the very slightest of winds. The vast sea of grass continues
to the west and south, while to the east the golden grass starts to give way to
brambles and hardier plants, which can grow happily in the rougher ground to be
found in this direction.
name: Golden Plains

desc:
Here there appear to be the faint remains of a trail - evidenced only by there
being slightly less bramble bushes in one area than another. Broken rocks and
uprooted vegetation lie all about. To the south the trail appears to become more
defined, while to the west the brush gives way to golden plains of luscious
grass. In the east lies a large chasm, effectively blocking any travel in that
direction.
name: Remains of a Trail

desc:
Decay has enveloped this road to such an extent that its destination is no
longer apparent. The ground is rough and great boulders sprawl out on the
terrain. Sharp brambles cover the ground, spreading from the large thicket to
the south, and making passage very difficult. The smell of rot and decay
permeates the air and casts a gloom over the land.
name: Destroyed Old Trail

desc:
The elements seem to have taken their toll on this path. A few boulders appear
to have been split and blackened by lightning while numerous cracks and
uprisings in the ground give evidence to some seismic activity. Tall weeds force
their way up among the cracks and brambles dot the uneven ground. The way bends
about, leading westwards, as a gaping chasm lies directly to the north.
name: Old Trail

desc:
The trail meanders among the plants, heading roughly north and south. Here and
there wild grasses obscure portions of the trail while a few scattered trees
offer nearby shelter from the elements. To the east, the ground rises up into a
flat plateau, while to the west a thick stand of brush and brambles prevents
easy travel in that direction.
name: Old Trail

desc:
The trail runs north and south, weaving about through the even terrain. A few
bushes and small trees here and there comprise the sparse plant life in the
area. The path contains numerous pockmarks which have been somewhat smoothed
over by time and weather. By the side of the path a small sign is posted
pointing to the east.
name: Old Trail

desc:
The trail is wide here, leading north and south over dry grassland which is
sparsely populated with trees. At this point the trail bends around a large
black pillar of roughly hewn stone which abruptly stands in the middle of the
path. Few trees grow in this area making the pillar easily visible from a
significant distance.
name: Old Trail

desc:
To the sides of the trail, the remnants of many fires and camps attest to the
fact that this path was, at one time, a well travelled one. A stretch of the
hardened trail here contains a few discarded horseshoes, a broken wagon wheel, a
broken crate, and discarded lanterns, which all together are evidence of the
high rate of travel, if not messiness, that occurred along this trail.
name: Old Trail

desc:
The grass here is greener and the soil damper than that of the golden brown
fields to your west. To the north the path leads away in the marshes. In the
distance the faint sound of rushing water drifts on the breeze.
name: Path through the Wetlands

desc:
As the hard dirt trail heads north and south through the sparse land, small
signs of past civilisation can be seen about the road. A decayed wooden wall
stands to the side of the road, bravely remaining upright while its three other
partners lie at its base, broken and rotten. To the east of the road, a dark
patch of trees and bushes can be seen.
name: Old Trail

desc:
The surface of the roadway becomes soft and wet as wild green grass overruns
part of the roadway. Ferns growing on both sides of the trail expand high into
the air, overhanging some parts of the trail. To the east, thick ferns comprise
a dark green clump of vegetation.
name: Old Trail

desc:
Cool air wafts in from the east as the sound of splashing water emanates from
the same direction. Looking east, the river can be seen running near the trail,
its banks only several meters from the trail. Between the river and the trail,
trees line the river's banks.
name: Old Trail

desc:
The trail continues north and south. Parts of the path have been deepened with
ruts while other sections are littered with debris such as broken crates and
miscellaneous parts of wagons. Though there is much debris, the rusted and
decayed condition of much of the litter is an indication that it has been a long
time since this way was regularly used.
name: Old Trail

desc:
The trail fades here, at the shores of the lake to the south. Trees stand just
beyond the shores of the lake which extends to the southwest and southeast. The
hard packed dusty ground of the trail is a sharp contrast to the soft wet shore
of the lake. To the east, a river can be seen emptying into the lake.
name: Old Trail

desc:
This part of the path is very overgrown with some grass and flowers. It is hard
to see any signs of humans passing this way for quite some time. The path
continues to the west and becomes hardly outlined in the south, while to the
east a very dense patch of brambles creates an impassable barrier.
name: Path through the Wetlands

desc:
The ground here is relatively dry, and covered with thick golden grass. The
rolling fields continue in all directions, as far as the eyes can see. From the
west the faint sound of rushing water reaches your ears.
name: Golden Plains

desc:
The ground here is solid, covered by a golden species of grass that glows in the
sunlight and sways peacefully in the breeze. The grasslands seem to continue in
the west. To the south the ground becomes slightly more green in hue, while to
the east stands a very dense patch of brambles and thorns.
name: Golden Plains

desc:
Dark and gloomy, large bramble bushes surround you, reaching out with their
thorns to grab at your clothes and rip your flesh. Weaving to and fro in the
slightest breeze, it almost seems as if the bushes resent the intrusion into
their private sanctuary. Closing in all around you, the only ways out are back
the way you came, or to fight your way through another patch of tangled, sharp
branches.
name: Dense Brambles

desc:
The thick thorns and brambles here make progress painstakingly difficult. Sharp
and thorny growth chokes out all other life, strangling the grasses and the
trees of light and room in the soil. The dense bramble thicket creates an almost
impassable barrier to the north, even forcing the trail in the east to curve
round it.
name: Bramble Thicket

desc:
Tall weeds push their way up through the cracked soil, some rising high enough
to obscure a fully grown man or possibly even an elf. Some vines armed with
small thorns grow in the scattered bushes. Small black berries hang from them
but appear too little to be of any nourishment. Holes dug into the rough ground
signal the presence of some animal life.
name: Tall Weedy Grounds

desc:
After a moderate rise, the ground becomes very level, allowing a grand view of
the surrounding land. There is little growth here aside from a few scrubby trees
which ruggedly survive on this unwelcoming ground. A thick layer of light brown
dust covers the ground, sometimes swirling around at a puff of the wind.
name: Dusty Mesa

desc:
This is a very small peak, if one at all, for it only rises thirty or forty feet
above the ground. Nevertheless, it provides a clear and broad view of the
surrounding area. An examination of the lands to the south reveals the presence
of a river and a trail. Brambles and bushes can be seen to the north and west
while some trees can be seen in the eastern distance.
name: Small Peak

desc:
Large bushes with long thorns crowd the terrain. Progress through here is
delicate and slow due to the sharp nature of the thorns. Though the ground is
relatively flat here, footing is still treacherous and rightly demands much
attention as fallen thorns are a lurking danger.
name: Thick Berry Bushes

desc:
In contrast to the thick and thorny bushes to the north and west, the plants
here are lush, green, and leafy. The river bank is here, and the glimmering
waters can be seen flowing to the east and south side of the clearing. The
ground is soft and damp, making passage very comfortable.
name: River Clearing

desc:
Short trees, no taller than six or seven yards, spread outwards, rather than up,
bearing thick wide leaves, covering up the sky above. The effect is made more
dismal from the climbers and vines that snake through the branches. A sweet
smell mixed with the scent of decay wafts through the area, in a mysterious
fashion. Certain areas of branches are broken as if something large had passed
through.
name: Shadowy Thicket

desc:
The area is quiet and calm as a cool mist hovers above the vegetation. The wet
and soft soil gives under the slightest weight, only becoming firm some six
inches below the surface of the ground. Thick ferns comprise much of the
plantlife here and seem to thrive in the wet ground and moist air. A fresh and
earthy smell from the greenery permeates the thick air. Clear drops of water can
be seen hanging under the fern leaves.
name: Thick Ferns

desc:
Thick sheltering branches lean over the banks, extending their boughs over the
bend in the river. The river appears smooth and all is quiet, at least for the
moment. The fresh and vibrant smell of wet leaves drift by as fallen vegatation
accelerates downstream.
name: Tributary of the Glanduin

desc:
The water is cold and rapid as the river flows southward. Despite much debris
floating on and carried in the current, the river does not seem to be hindered
or slowed by it at all. Little shallow pools form on the banks of the river
where the current is not so strong. To the east many trees can be seen.
name: Tributary of the Glanduin

desc:
So close to the outlet of a lake the river is very wide here, and the current is
so strong that it takes effort to overcome it. To the northwest you can see a
large hill and scouting southwards you can see a tower which overlooks the
surrounding lands. North of here lies a path of some sort. The river continues
to the west, and south of here lies an old road.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The lake is narrow here, and it continues westwards into the River Glanduin. The
water is light blue, but shallow here in the north-western corner of the lake.
Water lilies are growing along the banks, and a trail can be seen following the
northern shore. West and southwards the lake continues as far as the eye can
see.
name: End of the Lake

desc:
The south-western shore of the lake is surrounded by roads both on the western
and southern shore. The lake seems to be very deep, and the colour of the water
is deep blue. Not much seems to grow along the shores, but the area seems to be
an ideal place to fish. North and eastwards the lake continues as far as the eye
can see.
name: Wide Lake

desc:
The southern side of the lake is deep, and the water is green. A current coming
from the north is the reason for the ice-cold water. The shore consists mostly
of rocks, and access to and from the water is hard. A road runs along the
southern shore from east to west. No land can be seen north, east or west of
here
name: Wide Lake

desc:
Along the southern shore a road stretches from east to west. To the south-east a
bridge can be seen. The water here is dark and cold, and the shore is filled
with rocks which makes it hard to exit the water. At the bottom of the lake the
ground is also filled with rocks, making it uncomfortable to move compared to
the sandy shores at the northern side of the lake.
name: Wide Lake

desc:
The lake is at its widest here, and only the northern shore can be seen in the
distant horizon. Some trees can be noticed beyond the northern shore. In these
parts of the lake the water is dark blue and it seems like this is the deepest
part. In every direction besides north the lake seems to be without an end.
name: Wide Lake

desc:
Fresh water from a tributary pours into the lake from the north. Sand and gravel
from the outwash have collected in shifting bars and shoals. Solid ground is far
from here, however. The wind creates ruffles in the water, and longer swells
wash towards the distant ends of the lake. A slow current is set to the west.
Waterfowl wheel very high overhead, out of bowshot.
name: Wide Lake

desc:
The river widens and the rushing waters from the north slow from their crowded
and frenzied rush downstream as they begin to enter the lake. A shallow and soft
delta forms at the entrance of the river to the lake and many particles of dirt
can be seen swirling in the delta's waters. In the east and west the lake
retreats to the south a bit leaving broad sandy shores bordered by many trees
behind them.
name: Tributary of the Glanduin

desc:
The fields are in good condition here, fertile and moist, yet not soggy or
overgrazed. To the north the grasslands seem to continue in an ocean of swaying
green foliage, while to the south an old stone path leads down towards the
river.
name: Rolling Grasslands

desc:
An ancient road lies before you, carefully crafted of individual stones woven
together in a solid, intricate pattern. The skill and labour obviously put into
the construction of this road has paid off, for it remains straight and flat
even after many obvious years of ageing.
name: Path of Stone

desc:
Small pebbles and dark wet sand line the shores of the southern lake, which from
this distance looks very calm and inviting. The air is fresh and the sound of
birds carry across from the other side of the lake. To the north a grove of
trees can be seen growing on the banks of a river which flows into the lake just
to the west.
name: Lakeshore

desc:
The shore stretches westwards along the northern coast of the lake which appears
to be fed by a river emptying into it to the west. Grasses and some weeds
encroach on the shore from the north, some patches reaching all the way to the
waters edge. In the distance to the north, a tall rock cliff can be seen.
name: Lakeshore

desc:
The river bends and twists as it makes its way downstream, following its course
passionately, frothing and tumbling on itself. The water flows with a power that
easily sweeps any debris with it as the large logs and branches in the current
attest to. A slight rumble can be heard from the river as its strength begins to
increase as it makes its way west.
name: Tributary of the Glanduin

desc:
The river's waters speed along, splashing, swirling, and overflowing over
anything in its path, as it hurries along downstream. The river bends around to
the south, curving around a small sandy peninsula. As the river swings to the
south, its inertia causes some of its waters to overflow westwards, creating a
swampy area inhabited by lush ferns to the west. The river becomes a little
muddled to the southwest as swamp waters rejoin the river there.
name: Tributary of the Glanduin

desc:
The river deepens, as the water becomes darker and the bottom is no longer
visible. Apparently the current of the river has also picked up as the edges
have become brown as the rapid water has picked up more and more dirt from the
banks. A few leaves speedily floating down the river are another indication of
the increased velocity of the water.
name: Tributary of the Glanduin

desc:
The river bends here, becoming shallow at the same time but now flowing
southwards. At the banks of the river reeds and tall grasses sprout up through
the water's surface, creating a strip of marshy ground. The water itself is very
clear and the bottom of the river is distinctly visible at the shallow parts of
the river.
name: River Lined with Reeds

desc:
A few small and slender forms can be seen darting to and fro under the shaded
surface of the river along the banks. An assortment of rocks and cobbles
decorate the river bed as the crystal clear water advances westwards. Far
upstream to the east, the river is shallow and narrow as a patchwork dam,
apparently made of timbers and branches, can be seen far in the eastern horizon.
name: Peaceful River

desc:
Tall rocky cliffs rise up all about. Broken rocks lie strewn all over the
ground. Here and there are rocks which appear to have been shaped by hands
rather than nature. At the base of several of the cliffs, portions of rock have
been gouged out leaving small alcoves in the cliffs.
name: Abandoned Quarry

desc:
The ground here is elevated some twenty to thirty feet above the level terrain.
The ground is rocky but firm and a few hardy trees grow upon this gusty mound.
To the north and west the ground abruptly erodes straight down into the banks of
a rushing river. Slowly, the high ground slopes down to the east while to the
south it becomes rocky.
name: Tranquil Outlook

desc:
Lush healthy apple trees grow on this small peninsula which is surrounded by the
River Glanduin. It appears this was once a cultivated orchard as the orderly
rows of trees attest to. Still the enriching river with its alluvial soil and
clear waters has taken over the task of nourishing this grove of trees. A large
rock cliff stands to the east, preventing any passage in that direction.
name: Wild Orchard

desc:
Prickly leaved bushes encompass much of the area, making passage a scratchy
experience. Brambles overrun much of the open ground making the terrain even
more treacherous. To the west the land rises sharply while to the north, the
thick bushes gradually thin out.
name: Thick Brake

desc:
Some trees have grown together here into a small bushy island with the river on
one side, and flatter land on the other. A thin carpet of grass covers the
ground underneath the trees. The ground slopes down to the north as it reaches
towards the banks of a river.
name: Sparse Thicket

desc:
A few sparsely leaved saplings and some thorny bushes dot the rocky and hilly
terrain. Other smaller and thornier bushes cover much of the ground, leaving
very little room to manoeuvre through. Here and there holes bore into the
ground, betraying the existence of some wildlife.
name: Thick Scrub

desc:
Here, the ground begins to dip significantly, angling down towards the river to
the south. Colourful mosses, lichens, and wildflowers blossom and sprout
inbetween and out from under boulders and their damp and soily undersides. The
moisture on the rock surface of the ground makes footing treacherous and the
prospect of a fall into the river more apparent, if not more likely.
name: Green Valley

desc:
The slope of the valley begins to level out as the valley starts to give way to
flat terrain again. Bushes and tall weeds have a larger presence here, yet
numerous colorful flowers still sprout up occasionally. On a few rockfaces near
the river, moss and lichen can be seen thriving. The moisture and the hard rock
surfaces add much danger and difficulty to the footing throughout this area.
name: Flattening Valley

desc:
The area is populated by low trees whose branches hang so low that the trees
look like large bushes. The terrain is comprised of a series of small mounds
which make passage somewhat tougher than usual. The ground itself is dusty and
rocky with little else growing save for the grove of low trees which are rooted
in between some of the mounds.
name: Bushy Grove

desc:
A hard packed stretch of ground is the only remaining semblance of a trail which
is intermittently overrun and covered by weeds and other plant life. The path
heads roughly east and west, however it disappears on hard dry ground to the
east and ends at the foot of a small mesa to the west. Small loose rocks on the
trail make footing uncertain at times.
name: Deserted Path

desc:
The ground is ripped and torn asunder as if a series of tremors had ravaged the
land, or as if some huge creature had awoken from the depths and angrily made
its way up through the surface. It is not certain if either may be the case,
however the small sharp hills and the trees now growing out at odd angles from
the uprooted ground are an indication that this area has undergone a violent
change.
name: Cratered Surface

desc:
The bushes and small plants here are very sickly. The grass blades and leaves on
other plants are yellow, instead of healthy green, and dark brown spots can be
seen clustering on some leaves. The soil itself does not appear rich and lively
but rather pale and sandy. The terrain changes into a flat and smooth plain to
the east while it turns very rocky to the west.
name: Wasteland

desc:
The land relaxes and flattens out into a gentle plain. Compared to the tall
grasses and thick scrub growing to the west and the east, this low plain seems
very silent and peaceful. Small grasses on the ground only reach as high as a
handspan and traces of animal life are either well hidden or, more likely,
nonexistent.
name: Quiet Plain

desc:
The terrain is uneven with small thorny bushes clustering in low areas. Cracks
in the soil are testimony of the severe lack of adequate water here. To the
south the land seems much the same while a small patch of grassland can be seen
to the east.
name: Thick Scrub

desc:
The tall grass here does not have the usual solid green colour typical of most
grasses. The blades are of a light brown tint and appear to be much thinner than
normal wild grasses. The dried out remains of grasses on the ground give
evidence to the harshness of life here.
name: Dry Grass

desc:
Many leafy bushes and small trees cover the ground, giving the area a distinctly
green and fresh aura. The soil is rich and dark under the lush tree branches
which sprout out in all directions from the tree trunks. The thickness of the
brush prevents any decent view of the areas in the distance.
name: Thick Brush

desc:
Many tall trees are rooted here upon the grassy slopes of what is a slight
valley among mostly even terrain. The ground slopes down from the east as the
valley sprawls westward. A thick soft layer of needles on the ground makes
progress through the valley more comfortable if not easier. Far to the south, a
glint of water can be seen at times.
name: Wooded Valley

desc:
A large deep pond has formed here as a dam of timbers and twigs holds up its
western end. Water wells up from some underground source, possibly a natural
spring, and distrurbs the otherwise flat surface of the pond, creating little
expanding wavelets. Water seeps over the dam and flows down westward as more
continues to gush forth from under the pond. On the grassy shores of the pond,
gnawed stumps of trees testify to the constitution of the dam, and the ingenuity
of indigenous wildlife.
name: Beaver Dam

desc:
The landscape is dotted with young saplings and a few young trees whose trunks
still bend at every breath of the wind. Among the trees stand numerous large
gnawed stumps. The ground is somewhat open as a few bushes encroach on the open
woodland. To the north is a large pond, the western edge of which appears to be
held up by a dam made of logs and mud.
name: Sparse Woodland

desc:
Round holly bushes seem to thrive in this area. The ground is spotted with
numerous fallen holly berries, which give it a bloody appearance. Curly leaves,
armed with small needles on their edges, on the holly bushes hamper progress
through the area while the fallen berries make footing very slippery. A large
road can be seen to the south.
name: Holly Copse

desc:
The terrain here is level and dry. Short brown grass covers the ground, looking
much like hay ready to be baled. Dust swirls up from the grassland, here and
there exposing the roots and runners of the grasses. The dry ground is
interrupted by bushes to the west and a road to the south.
name: Dry Grassland

desc:
Large leafy bushes cover the dark moist ground here. The prickly leaves of the
bushes make progress slow as there is very little room between any of the
bushes. Many berries grow on each bush though not all of the bushes bear the
same berries. The smell of the berries gives the air a strong sweet flavor.
name: Berry Grove

desc:
A faint trail, which in reality is just a slight way through the bushes, heads
east and west. A few old small prints in the ground seem to indicate that this
trail is used by animals, which apparently are heading to and from a water
source in the west as the glint of water can be seen far off in that direction.
name: Trail through the Brush

desc:
Trees populate this plain, giving it the look of a light forest. Tall pines
stand in small clusters, their needles scattered about to form an uneven blanket
over the grass. The grasses thrive among many of the trees, making the most of
the open ground and the dead needles on the ground. To the west, the frequency
of trees increases to form a wood.
name: Wooded Plains

desc:
Small bushes armed with many thorns carpet the rocky and otherwise barren
ground. The ground itself slopes up and down in an uneven fashion, leaving many
different sized mounds and depressions. To the east a large rock can be seen in
the distance while to the south the ground becomes lush and lively with thick
bushes and trees.
name: Thick Scrub

desc:
Small trees, bushes, and thorny vines comprise the bleak landscape. The thick
plantlife covering the area makes it very difficult to traverse as there is
little open ground to step on. Muffled rustling among the plants seems to
indicate the presence of small wildlife.
name: Thick Scrub

desc:
Wild tall grasses populate the area. The density and height of the grasses make
it hard to move through the area. Visibility is also hampered and it is
compounded by the fact that much attention must be paid to the tricky footing
among the grasses. The blades of the grasses are sharp and firm, making a trip
through the area an unpleasant, if not painful, one.
name: High Grasses

desc:
Thick grass is joined by ragged weeds and white and yellow wildflowers. Each
weed and wildflower grows as high if not higher than the tall grasses. To the
north the grassland continues while a large rock looms over the flat plains to
the south.
name: Wildflowers and Grasses

desc:
A huge mass of rock pushes its way up from the ground here. It probably stands
some twenty feet high and is at least forty feet long. The rock itself appears
to be of hard granite. Sturdy pines growing on the even ground nearby offer a
sharp contrast to the unusual rock formation. Small patches of weeds and grasses
grow among the pines.
name: Jutting Rock

desc:
Blackened stumps of what once apparently were great thick trees are strewn all
about. Dark ash covers much of the level ground, giving it a dead and rotting
appearance. A patch or two of grass can be seen here and there, however, no
saplings can be seen which would herald a possible rebirth of this wood.
name: Splintered Woods

desc:
Weeds, thorns, and brambles are interspersed among the wild grasses, clumping
into small thickets. Also intermingled among the grasses are patches of wild
honeysuckle and berry bushes, which enrich the dull green of the grasses with
dark and bright colours. The honeysuckle and berries give the air a natural
sweet scent. Trampled ground running east and west is the only indication of a
trail.
name: Tangled Trail

desc:
The land is flat all around and is sparsely covered with parched brown grasses.
A great tree stands here with its thick tall trunk making it appear as if it
were the only connection between the ground and the heavens. A large circular
area underneath the overhanging branches of the tree is sheltered from much of
the natural elements.
name: Underneath an Immense Tree

desc:
The ground rises up into a small plateau, giving limited view of the surrounding
area. Dust covers the ground and swirls up at every puff of the wind. The soil
is barren and devoid of any rich topsoil to support any plantlife. To the south
lies a road running east and west. A lone tree can be seen standing alone far
into the northen horizon.
name: Approach to the Road

desc:
Gallons of cold water plummet down from a high ledge some thirty feet above. As
the water falls through the air and crashes down onto the bottom, thick white
foam is formed at the surface and a dense mist spreads through the air. The
river bends southwards and the channel of the river widens, giving the waters
enough room to calm and slow. Slimy green moss coats many of the rocks lining
the sides of the river. A few trees can be seen bathing in the cool mist behind
the rocks.
name: At the Bottom of the Falls

desc:
The sparkling water speeds along, seeming to gather momentum as it continues
westward towards a large drop of some thirty feet straight down. At the edge of
the waterfall, the water level decreases and smoothed rocks can be seen on the
riverbed and jutting up and out of the river's surface. Large boughs from a few
sturdy trees growing near the ledge on the banks of the river extend out over
the waterfall.
name: Cascade of the River Glanduin

desc:
Shade-bearing green leafy trees cloak the river from both sides, their thick
branches drooping over the clear water. Occasional leaves and bits of bark are
bourne along near the turbulent surface of the river, bobbing and dipping up and
down all the while. The river swings to the west, slowing a bit as it rounds the
bend.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
Water rushes around the beginnings of a wooden dam which extends out from the
banks of the river. The water, itself, is cold and clear, and offers a good view
of the shallow bottom of the river. A few fallen trees remain lodged near an
eroding slope of clay, dirt, and rocks. Grass covers much of both banks, almost
up to the water's edge.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
Cold clean water sparkles with purity, allowing unobscured views of the smooth
stones on the river bottom below, and of the schools of large fish that swim
along. The river swirls and whirls over a few rocks that jut up above the
surface, foaming a bit as it continues its journey west.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The swirling current bends to the south, churning and foaming with increasing
fury. The waters of the river crash against the steep shores in the west as the
current attempts to turn southwards. At the top of the shores, stones line the
side of what appears to be a road which runs along the river to the west and
north.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The swirling brown water of the river sweeps powerfully along to the west,
accelerating here as if preparing to leap over some obstacle. Where it meanders
northward, a long, level embankment can be clearly seen, protected from the
river where necessary by stone breakwaters: the Road of the Smiths. Reeds grow
in the shallows and oaks, willows, and leafy shrubs line the banks.
name: Glanduin River

desc:
The broad river flows westward through an untenanted land. Along its banks are
willow trees, thick bushes, and reeds in which wading birds look for fish to
grab up. To the north, beyond the ancient road which parallels the river, low,
heath-covered hills rise somberly.
name: Glanduin River

desc:
The Glanduin flows westward toward distant Tharbad and its appointment with the
